description: Golden Q/A set over the ReAct paper (Yao et al.) corpus. evidence_any: retrieval hit if any top-k chunk contains any of these substrings (case/whitespace-insensitive). evidence_all: every listed substring must be found across the retrieved set (multi-chunk questions). answer_keys: substrings a correct answer must contain (any one of each inner list).

questions.question: What is ReAct and what does it prompt language models to do?
questions.reference_answer: ReAct is a paradigm that prompts LLMs to generate both verbal reasoning traces and task-specific actions in an interleaved manner, so reasoning helps the model plan and act, and actions let it gather information from external sources.
evidence_any: generate both verbal reasoning traces and actions pertaining to a task in an interleaved manner

questions.question: Which base language model was used for the main experiments in the ReAct paper?
questions.reference_answer: PaLM-540B (a frozen large language model), with some GPT-3 results in the appendix.
evidence_any: a frozen large language model, PaLM-540B

questions.question: On which four benchmarks was ReAct evaluated?
questions.reference_answer: HotpotQA (question answering), FEVER (fact verification), ALFWorld (text-based game), and WebShop (webpage navigation).

questions.question: By what absolute success rate does ReAct outperform imitation and reinforcement learning methods on ALFWorld and WebShop?
questions.reference_answer: By an absolute 34% on ALFWorld and 10% on WebShop.
evidence_any: absolute improvement of 34% and 10% in success rates

questions.question: What are the three action types in the Wikipedia web API designed for ReAct?
questions.reference_answer: search[entity] (returns first 5 sentences of the entity page or suggests similar entities), lookup[string] (returns the next sentence containing the string, like Ctrl+F), and finish[answer] (finishes the task with the answer).
evidence_any: which would return the next sentence in the page containing

questions.question: How many in-context exemplar cases were selected from the training sets for HotpotQA and for Fever?
questions.reference_answer: 6 cases for HotpotQA and 3 for Fever, manually composed into ReAct-format trajectories.

questions.question: In ReAct, what is a 'thought' and how does it differ from other actions?
questions.reference_answer: A thought (reasoning trace) is an action in the language space that does not affect the external environment and gets no observation feedback; it composes useful information by reasoning over the current context to support future reasoning or acting.
evidence_any: does not affect the external environment, thus leading to no observation feedback

questions.question: How many steps were ReAct trajectories limited to on HotpotQA and FEVER before backing off to CoT-SC?
questions.reference_answer: 7 steps for HotpotQA and 5 steps for FEVER.
evidence_any: 7 and 5 steps for HotpotQA and FEVER

questions.question: How many trajectories were used to finetune the smaller PaLM models, and how were they obtained?
questions.reference_answer: 3,000 trajectories with correct answers generated by ReAct itself (a bootstrapping approach similar to STaR), used to finetune PaLM-8B and PaLM-62B.

questions.question: What is ALFWorld?
questions.reference_answer: A synthetic text-based game aligned with the embodied ALFRED benchmark, with 6 task types where an agent achieves high-level household goals via text actions.
evidence_any: synthetic text-based game designed to align with the embodied ALFRED benchmark

questions.question: How many products and human instructions does the WebShop environment contain?
questions.reference_answer: 1.18 million real-world products and 12k human instructions.

questions.question: What frequent error pattern specific to ReAct was observed on HotpotQA?
questions.reference_answer: The model repetitively generates previous thoughts and actions, failing to reason its way out of the loop; this was categorized as reasoning error.
evidence_any: repetitively generates the previous thoughts and actions

questions.question: What percentage of ReAct's error cases on HotpotQA were caused by non-informative search results?
questions.reference_answer: 23% of the error cases.
evidence_any: counts for 23% of the error cases

questions.question: What were the false positive (hallucinated) success rates of CoT versus ReAct on HotpotQA?
questions.reference_answer: CoT had a 14% false positive rate versus 6% for ReAct.
evidence_any: much higher false positive rate than ReAct (14% vs. 6%)

questions.question: After finetuning with just 3,000 examples, how did PaLM-8B ReAct compare to larger prompted models?
questions.reference_answer: Finetuned PaLM-8B ReAct outperformed all PaLM-62B prompting methods, and finetuned PaLM-62B ReAct outperformed all PaLM-540B prompting methods.
evidence_any: PaLM-8B finetuned ReAct outperforming all PaLM-62B prompting methods

questions.question: Which prior work does the ReAct paper identify as the closest to ReAct in using an inner monologue for embodied agents, and what is its key limitation?
questions.reference_answer: Inner Monologue (Huang et al., 2022b); its 'inner monologue' is limited to observations of the environment state and what needs to be completed, lacking flexible high-level reasoning like goal decomposition and commonsense.
evidence_any: limited to observations of the environment state and what needs to be completed

questions.question: By how much did ReAct outperform the IM-style prompting ablation (ReAct-IM) on ALFWorld overall?
questions.reference_answer: 71 vs 53 overall success rate.

questions.question: What NSF grant number supported the ReAct work?
questions.reference_answer: National Science Foundation Grant No. 2107048.

questions.question: Summarize the main idea and headline results of this paper.
questions.reference_answer: The paper introduces ReAct, which interleaves reasoning traces and actions in LLMs for greater synergy; on HotpotQA and Fever it reduces hallucination and error propagation by interacting with a Wikipedia API, and on ALFWorld and WebShop it beats imitation/RL methods by 34% and 10% absolute success rate with only one or two in-context examples.
evidence_any: allowing for greater synergy between the two: reasoning traces help the model induce, track, and update action plans

questions.question: According to the abstract, on which tasks does ReAct overcome hallucination and error propagation issues of chain-of-thought reasoning, and how?
questions.reference_answer: On question answering (HotpotQA) and fact verification (Fever), by interacting with a simple Wikipedia API and generating human-like, interpretable task-solving trajectories.
evidence_any: overcomes prevalent issues of hallucination and error propagation in chain-of-thought reasoning by interacting with a simple Wikipedia API

questions.question: What HotpotQA exact-match score did the ReAct -> CoT-SC combined method achieve in Table 1?
questions.reference_answer: 35.1 EM on HotpotQA (the best prompting result on that task).

questions.question: In the ALFWorld task-specific results, what success rate did ReAct (best of 6) achieve on the Pick task?
questions.reference_answer: 92% on Pick.

questions.question: What score and success rate did ReAct achieve on WebShop, and how does that compare to the human expert?
questions.reference_answer: ReAct scored 66.6 with a 40.0% success rate; human experts scored 82.1 with 59.6% success.

questions.question: According to the failure-mode analysis table, what fraction of CoT failures were due to hallucination, and what fraction for ReAct?
questions.reference_answer: Hallucination accounted for 56% of CoT failures versus 0% for ReAct.

questions.question: How does the method let the model change course when something unexpected happens during a task?
questions.reference_answer: Reasoning traces (thoughts) let the model handle exceptions and adjust its action plans according to the situation, e.g. tracking progress and updating plans in language.
evidence_any: handle exceptions and adjust action plans
adjust the plan according to the situation

questions.question: Can a person step in and fix the agent's behavior while it is working on a task?
questions.reference_answer: Yes — humans can control or correct the agent's behavior on the go by editing its thoughts (thought editing), as shown in Figure 5.
evidence_any: control or correct the agent behavior on the go by thought editing

questions.question: Why is teaching a model to memorize facts considered worse than teaching it to look things up?
questions.reference_answer: Finetuning Standard or CoT essentially teaches models to memorize potentially hallucinated knowledge facts, whereas finetuning ReAct or Act teaches models how to reason and act to access information from Wikipedia — a more generalizable skill.
evidence_any: teaches models to memorize (potentially halluincated) knowledge facts

questions.question: What success rates did ReAct achieve on ALFWorld (best trial) and on WebShop, and against which baselines?
questions.reference_answer: On ALFWorld the best ReAct trial reached 71% average success (vs best Act 45% and BUTLER 37%); on WebShop ReAct reached a 40.0% success rate, about 10% absolute above the previous best IL/IL+RL methods.
evidence_all: 10% improvement over the previous best success rate

questions.question: What safety concerns do the authors raise about connecting language models to the outside world?
questions.reference_answer: Hooking an LLM to external environments has potential dangers such as looking up inappropriate or private information or taking harmful actions; their experiments limited interaction to safe websites (Wikipedia, WebShop) with no dangerous actions possible.
evidence_any: potential dangers, e.g. looking up inappropriate or private information

questions.question: Who is the first author of the ReAct paper and where was the work done?
questions.reference_answer: Shunyu Yao (Princeton University), with the work done during a Google internship in collaboration with Google Research, Brain team.

questions.question: What oxidation law do the HfCxN1-x ceramics follow at 1800 degrees C, and whose theory explains it?
questions.reference_answer: The oxidation kinetics basically follow a parabolic law, consistent with Wagner's theory, as shown by parabolic fits to the weight-gain data.
evidence_any: oxidation kinetics obey a parabolic law

questions.question: Which HfCxN1-x composition showed the best oxidation resistance at 1800 degrees C?
questions.reference_answer: HfC0.3N0.7 — oxidation rate constants decreased with increasing nitrogen content, and HfC0.3N0.7 had the minimum kp.
evidence_any: possessed the most superior oxidation resistance

questions.question: What anomalous oxidation behavior did the HfC0.7N0.3 sample exhibit?
questions.reference_answer: It deviated from the parabolic law: after 20 minutes of oxidation it showed rapid weight LOSS of −76.85 mg/cm², attributed to accelerated oxidation from oxide film spallation.

questions.question: Why did the SiB6-containing composite oxidize worse than the SiC-containing one?
questions.reference_answer: HfC0.5N0.5-20 vol% SiB6 generates the volatilizable product B2O3, which results in poorer oxidation behavior compared to HfC0.5N0.5-20 vol% SiC.

questions.question: What happens to the SiC-containing ceramic when oxidized at low air pressure?
questions.reference_answer: Low pressure leads to active oxidation of SiC: gaseous SiO forms and easily evaporates, gradually degrading the protective oxide layer — at 5 kPa the sample even lost weight (−2.56 mg/cm²).

questions.question: What weight gains did HfC0.5N0.5 show after oxidation at 1800 degrees C in 5, 10, and 20 kPa air?
questions.reference_answer: 9.34, 12.33, and 13.46 mg/cm² respectively — a clear trend of increasing weight gain with increasing air pressure.

questions.question: Which Hf1-xNbxC composition exhibited the best anti-oxidation property at 1700 degrees C for 30 h, and what oxide film did it form?
questions.reference_answer: Hf0.8Nb0.2C — it formed a dense oxide film of bulky HfO2 bonded by lathy Nb2Hf8O21.

questions.question: Which composition developed a free-standing single-phase HfO2 film under the oxyacetylene flame, and under what conditions?
questions.reference_answer: Hf0.9Nb0.1C developed a dense 'free-standing' single-phase HfO2 film under an oxyacetylene flame at 4.2 MW/m² for 40 s × 3, demonstrating excellent ablation resistance.

questions.question: In the OA-III oxyacetylene ablation system, what maximum surface temperatures correspond to heat fluxes of 2.4 and 4.2 MW/m²?
questions.reference_answer: 2.4 MW/m² gives Tmax of 2300 degrees C (120 s ablation); 4.2 MW/m² gives 2700 degrees C (40 s × 3).

questions.question: At what temperature does NbC oxidize into a liquid state in air?
questions.reference_answer: NbC oxidises into a liquid state at 1500 degrees C in air, and produces a dense filmy structure under an oxyacetylene flame in the 2000-2100 degrees C range.

questions.question: How does adding niobium carbide help hafnium carbide protect against extreme heat over a wide temperature range?
questions.reference_answer: NbC modification produces niobium oxides that bond and densify the HfO2 scale (e.g. Nb2Hf8O21/Nb2Hf6O17 phases), raising the antioxidant temperature limit (>=1700 C for Hf1-xNbxC with x<=0.2) and enabling dense protective films from static oxidation up to oxyacetylene ablation conditions.

questions.question: What three compositions did Singh et al. synthesize and by what method?
questions.reference_answer: (Zr,Ti)B2, (Zr,Ti,Ta)C, and dual-phase (Zr,Ti,Ta)C,B2, synthesized by spark plasma sintering at 1850 degrees C, 40 MPa for 30 min, achieving ~99% densification.

questions.question: How do the flexural strength and fracture toughness of the dual-phase (Zr,Ti,Ta)C,B2 compare to single-phase (Zr,Ti)B2?
questions.reference_answer: Dual phase: ~435 MPa flexural strength and 3.7 ± 0.3 MPa·m^1/2 toughness, versus ~212 MPa and 2.4 ± 0.3 MPa·m^1/2 for (Zr,Ti)B2 — a large enhancement, and very close to the single-phase carbide (~443 MPa).

questions.question: What are the boride and carbide phase fractions in the dual-phase (Zr,Ti,Ta)C,B2 composite?
questions.reference_answer: About 42% boride and 58% carbide.

questions.question: In the dual-phase composite, which elements segregate preferentially into which phase?
questions.reference_answer: Ta segregates primarily into the carbide phase (~49%) while Ti enriches into the boride phase (~44%); Zr distributes into both.

questions.question: What grain sizes were measured for (Zr,Ti)B2 versus the dual-phase composite?
questions.reference_answer: (Zr,Ti)B2 grains are ~12 um (12.0 ± 7.2), while the dual-phase (Zr,Ti,Ta)C,B2 is much finer at ~2.5 um (boride) / ~2.1 um (carbide) — microstructural refinement from the dual-phase design.

questions.question: Which institution carried out the study on NbC-modified HfC ceramics?
questions.reference_answer: Northwestern Polytechnical University (Carbon/Carbon Composites Research Center, Xi'an, China) — the He, Li, and Chang paper.

questions.question: Compare the sintering techniques and conditions used to densify the ceramics in these three studies (HfCxN1-x, Hf1-xNbxC, and the (Zr,Ti,Ta) boride-carbide).
questions.reference_answer: Chen et al. hot-pressed HfCxN1-x at 1950 C, 30 MPa, 1 h in argon; He et al. used vacuum hot-pressed sintering (VHPS, ~2000 C, 40 MPa); Singh et al. used spark plasma sintering at 1850 C, 40 MPa, 30 min.

questions.question: At what temperatures and durations were the hafnium-based ceramics oxidation-tested in the Chen and He studies?
questions.reference_answer: Chen et al. oxidized HfCxN1-x at 1800 C for 30 minutes (heated at ~30 C/s in graphite crucibles); He et al. oxidized Hf1-xNbxC in static air at 1700 C for 30 hours.

questions.question: What different additive strategies do these papers use to improve the oxidation resistance of HfC-based ceramics?
questions.reference_answer: Chen et al. use anionic modification — nitrogen doping (HfCxN1-x, resistance improves with N content) plus SiB6/SiC silicide additions that form molten SiO2/HfSiO4; He et al. use cationic modification — NbC additions whose niobium oxides bond and densify the HfO2 scale.

questions.question: In the macro photographs of the Chen paper (Fig. 10), what do the oxidized samples look like after 10, 20 and 30 minutes, and which sample's surface bulges?
questions.reference_answer: The photos show small square samples turned white by the oxide scale, arranged by composition and oxidation time on a red background; the HfC0.5N0.5-SiC and -SiB6 composites stay relatively smooth while pure HfC0.5N0.5's oxide layer bulges.

questions.question: Based on the optical images after 30 h oxidation at 1700 degrees C in the He paper (Fig. 4a), which samples show visible cracks and which look rough and porous?
questions.reference_answer: HfC and Hf0.9Nb0.1C show visible cracks in their oxide scales (HfC's runs across the surface); Hf0.7Nb0.3C and Hf0.6Nb0.4C appear rough and porous, while Hf0.8Nb0.2C shows a filmy surface with a central bulge.

questions.question: In the He paper's post-ablation optical images (Fig. 7), how does the HfC sample's surface differ from Hf0.9Nb0.1C after the 4.2 MW/m² test?
questions.reference_answer: The HfC disc is fractured by multiple large cracks across its surface, while Hf0.9Nb0.1C shows a more continuous grey-white oxide film with far less cracking (its film ruptures only locally).

questions.question: In the EDS elemental maps of the dual-phase composite in the Singh paper (Fig. 2), which elements show complementary spatial distributions and what does that indicate?
questions.reference_answer: Ta (yellow) and B (cyan) maps are complementary, and Ti co-locates with B: Ta preferentially segregates into the carbide phase and Ti enriches the boride phase, while Zr spreads across both — direct visual evidence of the dual-phase element partitioning.
